summary: This PR introduces a new `requests.JSONDecodeError` exception class and updates `response.json()` to re-raise decode failures as this type. While the motivation is sound, the implementation has several correctness bugs (wrong inheritance hierarchy, uncaught decode errors in one code path, Python 2 compatibility), security-relevant design issues (exception hierarchy broken relative to `RequestException`), and the new test file relies entirely on live network requests and unconditionally imports an optional dependency.

findings.title: All tests make live outbound network requests to external URLs
findings.detail: Every test in the new file hits real external URLs (`https://httpbin.org/get`, `https://google.com`). This means tests are non-deterministic, require internet access, cannot run in a CI environment without network, and exfiltrate environment metadata (IP, headers) to third-party services. Test outcomes can be influenced by external service availability or manipulation. Multiple `pytest.raises` blocks within a single test function each make a separate live HTTP call. The existing test suite uses local server fixtures or mocking (e.g. `requests_mock`, `pytest-httpserver`, or direct `Response` object construction) — all new tests should follow that pattern.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException, breaking the stated exception hierarchy
findings.detail: The PR description states that `requests.RequestException` will catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`, but the class definition `class JSONDecodeError(StandardJSONDecodeError, SimpleJSONDecodeError)` does not include `RequestException` in its bases. Its MRO is `JSONDecodeError -> json.JSONDecodeError -> ValueError -> Exception`, so `except requests.RequestException` will NOT catch it. This is both a correctness bug and a security-relevant concern: callers using a broad `except requests.RequestException` at an API boundary will find their handlers silently bypassed. There are no tests verifying `isinstance`/`issubclass` relationships with `RequestException`, and no `pytest.raises(RequestException)` test covering this catch path.

findings.title: Unconditional `import simplejson` breaks environments without simplejson installed
findings.detail: `import simplejson` at the top level will raise `ImportError` on any environment where simplejson is not installed, causing the entire test module to fail to import. Since one of the core goals of this PR is to handle the case where simplejson is NOT installed, there is no test coverage for that code path at all. The import should be guarded with `pytest.importorskip('simplejson')` or a `try/except ImportError` to match the rest of the codebase's treatment of simplejson as an optional dependency.

findings.title: json.JSONDecodeError in the UTF-detection branch is not caught and not re-raised as requests.JSONDecodeError
findings.detail: Inside the `if encoding is not None:` block, only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught (and silently `pass`-ed). A `json.JSONDecodeError` raised by `json.loads()` in that block propagates directly as a raw `json.JSONDecodeError`, not as `requests.JSONDecodeError`. Only the fallback `json.loads(self.text)` call at line 916 is wrapped in the new `try/except` that converts to `requests.JSONDecodeError`. This means the caller receives an inconsistent exception type depending on which code path is taken, violating the PR's stated guarantee. Additionally, the silent `pass` on `UnicodeDecodeError` can lead to a different JSON document being parsed by the fallback path (using chardet-based encoding), with no test covering either of these edge-case paths.

findings.title: Missing test coverage for key correctness claims and exception hierarchy properties
findings.detail: The test file has no coverage for: (1) `isinstance`/`issubclass` checks confirming `requests.JSONDecodeError` is a subclass of `json.JSONDecodeError`, `ValueError`, and `requests.RequestException`; (2) the code path where `guess_json_utf` finds an encoding but `json.loads` still raises `JSONDecodeError`; (3) that the re-raised exception preserves the original `msg`, `doc`, and `pos` attributes; (4) behavior when simplejson is absent. The success test only checks `isinstance(..., dict)`, which would also pass with the original code.

findings.title: Catching json.JSONDecodeError breaks on Python 2 where the attribute does not exist
findings.detail: `json.JSONDecodeError` was added in Python 3.5. On Python 2, the `except json.JSONDecodeError` clause will raise `AttributeError` at runtime, meaning any failed `json.loads` call on Python 2 will crash with `AttributeError` rather than raising `requests.JSONDecodeError`. The original code avoided this by using the `complexjson` alias or not catching at all. The fix is to either catch `ValueError` (the Python 2 equivalent) or use a compatibility alias in the except clause.

findings.title: Fallback `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` is semantically wrong and broadens exception scope
findings.detail: When simplejson is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is set to the root `Exception` class. Because `JSONDecodeError` inherits from `SimpleJSONDecodeError`, this is technically valid Python, but it is semantically wrong: the fallback adds nothing useful to the MRO (every exception already inherits from `Exception`), makes the MRO fragile, and could cause `__init__` resolution conflicts since `json.JSONDecodeError` requires `msg, doc, pos` positional args while `Exception`'s `__init__` is more permissive. More importantly, any code doing `except SimpleJSONDecodeError` when simplejson is absent would catch ALL exceptions. A private no-op sentinel base class (e.g., `class _NoSimpleJSONDecodeError(Exception): pass`) should be used as the fallback instead of bare `Exception`.

findings.title: Replacing `complexjson` alias with bare `json` import shadows stdlib name and local parameter
findings.detail: The previous `from .compat import json as complexjson` was intentional: it avoided shadowing both the stdlib `json` module name and the `json` keyword argument that appears in several method signatures in the same file (e.g. `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)` and `Request.__init__(..., json=None)`). Inside `prepare_body`, `json.dumps(json, ...)` is now ambiguous to readers — it relies on Python's scope resolution resolving `json` as the local parameter for the argument but the module for the attribute access. While it works today, it reduces readability and is a correctness risk for future edits.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError docstring style inconsistent with surrounding exceptions
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file begin with a capital letter and end with a period (e.g. `"""An HTTP error occurred."""`). The new class uses a lowercase first word and no period: `"""Couldn't decode the text into json"""`. It should also capitalise "JSON". Suggested: `"""A JSON decoding error occurred."""`

findings.title: Intermediate aliases StandardJSONDecodeError / SimpleJSONDecodeError leak into the public module namespace
findings.detail: `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` are module-level names that become importable from `requests.exceptions`, polluting the public API. They are only used as bases for `JSONDecodeError`. Consider prefixing them with an underscore (e.g. `_StandardJSONDecodeError`) or defining them inline to avoid namespace pollution, consistent with the existing style in this file.

findings.title: New `import json` breaks alphabetical/logical import ordering in the file
findings.detail: The existing code groups `import chardet` and `import sys` in a consistent order. The new `import json` is inserted before `import chardet`, breaking the alphabetical and logical grouping visible in the surrounding style. It should be placed alongside the other stdlib imports consistently.

findings.title: SimpleJSONDecodeError fallback to Exception breaks MRO and catch semantics
findings.detail: When simplejson is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is set to `Exception`. This means `JSONDecodeError` inherits from `Exception` as one of its bases. While that is technically valid, it makes `requests.JSONDecodeError` catchable by a bare `except Exception:` clause in a way that is indistinguishable from any other exception, but more importantly it violates the stated design: the fallback `Exception` is far too broad. Every exception in Python already inherits from `Exception`, so the `SimpleJSONDecodeError` base in the MRO adds nothing useful when simplejson is absent, yet it does make the MRO resolution fragile. More critically, when simplejson IS installed, `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` is a subclass of `ValueError`, so the MRO works fine; but when it is NOT installed and `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception`, `StandardJSONDecodeError` (which is `json.JSONDecodeError`, itself a subclass of `ValueError`) plus `Exception` produces a valid but semantically wrong hierarchy. The correct fallback for the "not installed" case should be a trivially-ignorable mixin (e.g., an internal no-op exception base), not `Exception` itself, to avoid accidentally swallowing unrelated exceptions and to keep `isinstance` checks clean.

findings.title: JSON decode error inside the UTF-detection branch is silently swallowed
findings.detail: Inside the `if encoding is not None:` block (lines 904-913), a `json.JSONDecodeError` raised by `json.loads()` is NOT caught. It propagates directly as `json.JSONDecodeError`, not as `requests.JSONDecodeError`. Only the fallback `json.loads(self.text, **kwargs)` call (line 916) has the new `try/except` wrapping. So if `guess_json_utf` finds an encoding and `json.loads` fails to decode, the caller receives a raw `json.JSONDecodeError` instead of the promised `requests.JSONDecodeError`. The fix is to also catch `json.JSONDecodeError` in the inner try block (or let it fall through to the outer try rather than just catching `UnicodeDecodeError`).

findings.title: json.JSONDecodeError catch breaks on Python 2 where only ValueError is raised
findings.detail: On Python 2, `json.JSONDecodeError` does not exist (it was added in Python 3.5). The `except json.JSONDecodeError` on line 917 will raise an `AttributeError` at runtime on Python 2, meaning any failed `json.loads` call on Python 2 will crash with `AttributeError` rather than raising the intended `requests.JSONDecodeError`. The original code avoided this by using the `complexjson` alias (which could be simplejson, which does have `JSONDecodeError`) or by not catching at all. The fix requires guarding: either catch `ValueError` (the Python 2 equivalent) or use a compat alias in the except clause.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException, breaking the stated hierarchy
findings.detail: The PR description says `requests.JSONDecodeError` and its parent `requests.RequestException` will both catch these errors, but `JSONDecodeError` does not inherit from `RequestException` at all. Its MRO is `JSONDecodeError -> json.JSONDecodeError -> ValueError -> Exception`. A `except requests.RequestException` will NOT catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`. This contradicts the stated design and the docstring/PR description. If the intent is for `RequestException` to be a parent, the class definition needs to include it explicitly.

findings.title: Name collision: local `json` import shadows the stdlib `json` module name
findings.detail: The line `from .compat import json` imports the stdlib `json` module under the name `json` into `models.py`. Previously it was aliased as `complexjson` to avoid shadowing. Now the same name `json` is used both for the module and as a parameter name in `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)` (line 460) and in `Request.__init__` (line 231). Inside `prepare_body`, `json.dumps(json, allow_nan=False)` on line 476 calls `json.dumps` where `json` is the parameter (a user-supplied object), not the module — this code path works only by accident because Python resolves `json` as the local parameter first for the argument but uses the module-level `json` for `.dumps`. However this is a pre-existing issue that the rename from `complexjson` to `json` makes more confusing and potentially fragile if the scope resolution ever changes, and is a correctness risk if someone reads or modifies this code.

findings.title: simplejson import fallback silently broadens exception catch scope to all exceptions
findings.detail: When simplejson is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is aliased to the root `Exception` class (line 19). Because `JSONDecodeError` inherits from `SimpleJSONDecodeError`, the new `requests.JSONDecodeError` itself becomes a subclass of `Exception` in a way that is expected — but more dangerously, any code that does `except SimpleJSONDecodeError` (e.g., a library consumer who directly referenced it) would catch ALL exceptions. The more immediate risk is in the MRO: if `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception`, then `JSONDecodeError(StandardJSONDecodeError, Exception)` means the `__init__` of `json.JSONDecodeError` (which requires `msg, doc, pos` positional args) may conflict or be bypassed via the Exception base during construction in edge cases. This could cause unexpected failure modes or, in a scenario where an attacker can influence whether simplejson is importable (e.g., via a malicious package in the path that raises ImportError), silently degrade the exception hierarchy. Consider using a private sentinel base class instead of `Exception` as the fallback.

findings.title: Test file makes live outbound network requests to external third-party URLs
findings.detail: The test file makes real HTTP requests to `https://httpbin.org/get` and `https://google.com`. From a security perspective this is problematic: (1) A CI/CD pipeline running these tests exfiltrates environment metadata (IP, headers, timing) to third-party services. (2) The test outcome is dependent on the availability and response of external services, which can be manipulated (DNS poisoning, BGP hijack, httpbin.org compromise) to make tests pass or fail in unexpected ways — including potentially causing a malicious response to be parsed as valid JSON and masking a real failure. (3) `import simplejson` at line 2 is an unconditional hard dependency; if simplejson is absent the entire test module fails to import, which could suppress other test failures in the suite. Tests should use mocking/fixtures instead of live network calls.

findings.title: UnicodeDecodeError from the early UTF-detection path is silently swallowed and not re-raised as JSONDecodeError
findings.detail: In the `json()` method, if `encoding` is detected and `json.loads` succeeds in the `try` block at line 904-906 it returns early. However if a `UnicodeDecodeError` is raised (line 908), it is caught and silently `pass`-ed, and execution falls through to the second `json.loads` at line 916. This means an attacker-controlled response body that is crafted to trigger a `UnicodeDecodeError` on the first path but then produce a different (possibly malicious) parse result on the fallback path (using `self.text` which uses chardet for detection) could cause a different JSON document to be parsed than expected. This is an existing behaviour, but the refactor now makes the fallback path the only place where `JSONDecodeError` is raised, so the inconsistency between the two code paths is now more visible. The `UnicodeDecodeError` should ideally also be wrapped in `JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException, breaking the requests exception hierarchy
findings.detail: All other network/response-related errors in the library inherit from `RequestException`. The new `JSONDecodeError` only inherits from `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError`. This means that a blanket `except requests.RequestException` will NOT catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`, which is a security-relevant concern: callers who rely on a single broad catch at an API boundary to prevent exception-driven information leakage (e.g., raw JSON parse details bubbling up through an application) will find their handlers bypassed. The PR description claims `requests.RequestException` will catch it, but the code does not support this.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError docstring style inconsistent with surrounding exceptions
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file use a capital letter and end with a period in their docstrings, e.g. `"""An HTTP error occurred."""`. The new class uses a lowercase first letter and no period: `"""Couldn't decode the text into json"""`. Should be something like `"""A JSON-related error occurred."""` to match the style of InvalidJSONError (`"""A JSON error occurred."""`), and "json" should be capitalised to "JSON".

findings.title: Intermediate aliases SimpleJSONDecodeError / StandardJSONDecodeError leak into module namespace
findings.detail: The names `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` are module-level names that become importable from `requests.exceptions`. The existing code style keeps only meaningful public names at module level. These are only used as bases for `JSONDecodeError`; they could be defined inline or built via a helper to avoid polluting the namespace.

findings.title: Fallback for missing simplejson uses bare Exception as a base class
findings.detail: `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` means `JSONDecodeError` inherits from `Exception` when simplejson is not installed. That silently broadens the MRO. A more intentional sentinel (e.g. a private dummy class) would be clearer about intent and consistent with the approach taken for `StandardJSONDecodeError` which uses `ValueError`.

findings.title: Import alias `complexjson` replaced with bare `json`, shadowing the built-in and a local parameter
findings.detail: The previous alias `from .compat import json as complexjson` avoided shadowing both the stdlib `json` module name and the `json` keyword argument that appears in several method signatures in this same file (e.g. `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)` and `__init__(…, json=None)`). The new `from .compat import json` reintroduces the shadowing risk. The alias `complexjson` was intentional; dropping it reduces readability in those methods.

findings.title: Import order: `import json` placed before `import chardet`, inconsistent with surrounding block
findings.detail: The existing untouched code has `import chardet` then `import sys`. The new `import json` is inserted before `import chardet`, which breaks the alphabetical/logical grouping visible in the surrounding style. Standard library imports should be grouped consistently; placing it after `import sys` (or before, but alongside the other stdlib imports) would be more consistent.

findings.title: Blank line removed inside `if is_py2` block without clear reason
findings.detail: The blank line after `from collections import Callable, Mapping, MutableMapping, OrderedDict` was removed. While minor, this is a style-only change unrelated to the JSON fix and diverges from the surrounding block structure visible in the file.

findings.title: Test file imports `simplejson` unconditionally, breaking environments without it
findings.detail: `import simplejson` at the top of the test file will raise `ImportError` on any environment where simplejson is not installed. The existing codebase handles simplejson as an optional dependency; the test should guard the import (e.g. `pytest.importorskip('simplejson')` or a `try/except`) to match that convention.

findings.title: Tests make real outbound network requests
findings.detail: The existing test suite uses local/mock servers (e.g. `httpbin` fixture, `pytest-httpserver`, or similar) rather than live URLs like `https://httpbin.org/get` and `https://google.com`. Relying on real external URLs makes tests flaky and slow, and is inconsistent with the project's testing conventions.

findings.title: Multiple `pytest.raises` blocks in one test function each make a separate network call
findings.detail: `test_json_decode_failure_catch` calls `get(failure_url).json()` three separate times (once per `pytest.raises` block), each making a live HTTP request. This should be restructured to fetch the response once and assert on it, consistent with how other tests in the project are written.

findings.title: Tests make live network requests instead of using mocks/fixtures
findings.detail: All tests hit real external URLs (https://httpbin.org/get and https://google.com). This means tests are non-deterministic, require network access, and cannot run in a CI environment without internet. The new JSON error-handling logic in models.py should be tested with mocked/local responses (e.g., using `requests_mock`, a local server fixture, or constructing a `Response` object directly). The existing test suite (test_requests.py, test_testserver.py) uses local server or mocking patterns that should be followed here.

findings.title: Test file unconditionally imports simplejson, making it fail when simplejson is not installed
findings.detail: `import simplejson` at the top level will cause an ImportError and all tests in this file will be skipped/errored when simplejson is not installed. Since the whole point of this PR is to handle the case where simplejson is NOT installed, this import should be conditional (e.g., `pytest.importorskip('simplejson')`). There is no test coverage for the code path where simplejson is absent.

findings.title: UnicodeDecodeError path in json() does not raise requests.JSONDecodeError — no test coverage
findings.detail: When `encoding` is not None and `json.loads` of the decoded content raises a `UnicodeDecodeError`, execution falls through silently (the `pass` at line 913) to the outer `json.loads`. However, if the outer `json.loads` also fails, it is now caught and re-raised as `requests.JSONDecodeError`. The `UnicodeDecodeError` silent-pass branch followed by a JSON failure is a distinct edge-case path with no test exercising it.

findings.title: json() encoding-detection branch (non-None encoding path) does not catch/re-raise as JSONDecodeError — no test coverage
findings.detail: When `guess_json_utf` returns a non-None encoding and `json.loads` raises a `json.JSONDecodeError` (not a UnicodeDecodeError), that exception is NOT caught and re-raised as `requests.JSONDecodeError`; it propagates as the raw `json.JSONDecodeError`. Only the fallback `json.loads(self.text, **kwargs)` call is wrapped in the try/except that converts to `requests.JSONDecodeError`. There is no test covering the case where the content-based decode path throws a `JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: No tests for JSONDecodeError exception hierarchy / isinstance relationships
findings.detail: The new `JSONDecodeError` class inherits from both `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` with conditional fallbacks. There are no unit tests verifying that `requests.JSONDecodeError` is actually an instance of `json.JSONDecodeError`, `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`, `ValueError`, and `RequestException` under the various import scenarios. The PR description claims all five types will be caught, but no test checks `isinstance` or `issubclass` relationships for `RequestException`.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException — no test verifies catch via RequestException
findings.detail: `class JSONDecodeError(StandardJSONDecodeError, SimpleJSONDecodeError)` does NOT inherit from `RequestException`, yet the PR description claims it can be caught via `requests.RequestException`. There is no test exercising catching a `requests.JSONDecodeError` as a `RequestException`. This specific catch path is entirely untested.

findings.title: test_json_decode_success only asserts the result is a dict, not that the decode actually succeeded with the new code path
findings.detail: The success test only checks `isinstance(..., dict)` which would also pass with the old code. There is no test asserting that a valid JSON response with a specific known payload is decoded correctly, nor any test that the new `json` (stdlib) import is used instead of `complexjson`/`simplejson`.

findings.title: No test for catching requests.JSONDecodeError as requests.RequestException
findings.detail: The PR description states that `requests.RequestException` should also catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`, but there is no `pytest.raises(RequestException)` test case in the test file.

findings.title: No test for the specific attributes (msg, doc, pos) preserved in the re-raised JSONDecodeError
findings.detail: The new raise `JSONDecodeError(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos)` preserves the original error's position and message. There is no test asserting that the re-raised exception carries the correct `msg`, `doc`, and `pos` attributes from the original parse failure.

findings.title: All tests make live outbound network requests to external URLs
findings.detail: Every test in the new file hits real external URLs (`https://httpbin.org/get`, `https://google.com`). This means tests are non-deterministic, require internet access, cannot run in a CI environment without network, and exfiltrate environment metadata (IP, headers) to third-party services. Test outcomes can be influenced by external service availability or manipulation. Multiple `pytest.raises` blocks within a single test function each make a separate live HTTP call. The existing test suite uses local server fixtures or mocking (e.g. `requests_mock`, `pytest-httpserver`, or direct `Response` object construction) — all new tests should follow that pattern.
